coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DH), as a kind of "lean meat powder", is easily accumulated in animal tissues and further endangers the health of consumers. As a result, developing a quick, sensitive, and cost-effective approach for detecting DH concentration is critical to food safety. In this paper, fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CQDs) peroxidase mimetic enzymes with remarkable stability and high catalytic efficiency were produced utilizing a green synthesis technique from three types of food waste: corncob, sunflower seed husk, and grape seed. Then a colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensor based on fluorescent CQDs was developed for the visual detection of dopamine hydrochloride in pork through changing the fluorescence brightness and color caused by its catalysis. The fluorescent CQDs generated from the three wastes were characterized, and the ones based on corncob performed the best as mimicked enzymes. Therefore, corncob fluorescent CQDs (CFCD) were used for the detection of DH. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range of DH measured by the fluorescent technique was 0.001–0.6 mmol/L (R 2 = 0.9947), with a detection limit of 7.86 nmol/L. The linear range of DH measured by the colorimetric technique was 0.001–0.7 mmol/L (R 2 = 0.9952), with a detection limit of 0.93 μmol/L. And with its high sensitivity and selectivity, it was successfully applied to the detection of DH in real samples. The developed CFCD concept may provide a novel method for the detection of DH. This comprehensive strategy promotes the high-efficiency conversion of biomass into CQDs and provides a theoretical basis for exploring the treatment of biomass waste.

head.ce:title: Biomass carbon quantum dots: Mimicking peroxidase-like activity and sensitive fluorometric and colorimetric detection of dopamine hydrochloride in meat products

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DH), as a kind of "lean meat powder", is easily accumulated in animal tissues and further endangers the health of consumers. As a result, developing a quick, sensitive, and cost-effective approach for detecting DH concentration is critical to food safety. In this paper, fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CQDs) peroxidase mimetic enzymes with remarkable stability and high catalytic efficiency were produced utilizing a green synthesis technique from three types of food waste: corncob, sunflower seed husk, and grape seed. Then a colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensor based on fluorescent CQDs was developed for the visual detection of dopamine hydrochloride in pork through changing the fluorescence brightness and color caused by its catalysis. The fluorescent CQDs generated from the three wastes were characterized, and the ones based on corncob performed the best as mimicked enzymes. Therefore, corncob fluorescent CQDs (CFCD) were used for the detection of DH. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range of DH measured by the fluorescent technique was 0.001–0.6 mmol/L (R 2 = 0.9947), with a detection limit of 7.86 nmol/L. The linear range of DH measured by the colorimetric technique was 0.001–0.7 mmol/L (R 2 = 0.9952), with a detection limit of 0.93 μmol/L. And with its high sensitivity and selectivity, it was successfully applied to the detection of DH in real samples. The developed CFCD concept may provide a novel method for the detection of DH. This comprehensive strategy promotes the high-efficiency conversion of biomass into CQDs and provides a theoretical basis for exploring the treatment of biomass waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DH) serves as a precursor to clenbuterol and is commonly used as a feed supplement for animals. Its inclusion in animal diets has been found to increase feed consumption and promote protein synthesis (Channer et al., 2023; Elizarova et al., 2022). It belongs to the same neurotransmitter group as substances such as catecholamines and phenylethylamines, and it plays an essential role in the brain and the human body (Grant et al., 2022). However, the presence of residues of DH in animal diets poses a significant risk to human health. Therefore, it is important to identify a highly sensitive, selective, simple, and reliable analytical method for the practical detection of DH (Al-Salahi, Hashem, & Abdel-Kader, 2022).

Thus far, various methods toward the detection of DH have been reported, such as high performance liquid chromatography (Neha, Mishra, Rani, Verma, & Sahoo, 2022), chemiluminescence method (Li, Zhang, Li, & Qi, 2022), spectrophotometric method (Ramadan, Almasri, & Khayal, 2020), electrochemical method (Nosratzehi et al., 2022), and fluorescence method etc (Zhang, Wang, et al., 2023). Fluorescence spectroscopy is distinguished by its high sensitivity, simple instruments, rapid analysis, and simplicity (Sun, Wang, Tang, Zhang, & Zhang, 2021). Using its color system visible to the unaided eye, colorimetry could provide a rapid determination of DH level (Du et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, a dual-read fluorescence and colorimetric assay for DH may utilize the benefits of both to produce a satisfactory detection method. This approach yields more convincing results than conventional single-signal sensing platforms.

Horseradish peroxidase catalyzes the production of ∙OH from H2O2, which causes the substrate to change color (Li, Li, et al., 2022). In this regard, it can discolor the color-developing solution because of the reducing property of DH. The change in color of the substrate was used to determine the content of DH (Zhang, Yu, et al., 2023). However, horseradish peroxidase and substrate and their related products were not fluorescent and horseradish peroxidase was easily inactivated and costly. Peroxidase mimics seem to be the best choice (Martins et al., 2023; Zhang, Tan, et al., 2023). To improve this problem, the mock enzymes in the study should exhibit fluorescent properties in addition to simulating enzyme catalytic activity.

The introduction of nanozymes (mimetic enzymes), a novel type of synthetic enzyme, has expanded the scope of simulated enzymes from organic molecules to inorganic nanomaterials (Xiao et al., 2022). The surface of nanozymes made of carbon quantum dot (CQDs) contains a large number of amine groups, carboxyl groups and polar groups, making them water-soluble and easily functionalized. Due to its chemical inertness and the fact that it can be synthesized using a large-scale, one-step method with greater biocompatibility than conventional quantum dots for biological, pharmaceutical, and food safety, it has become a substitute for conventional quantum dots and other nanozymes in many application scenarios since its discovery (Han et al., 2022). In particular, CQDs were used as a novel nanomimetic enzyme due to their fluorescent properties and peroxidase activity (Dhandapani et al., 2023). Compared to other nanozymes, CQDs have found extensive application in biochemical analysis, food analysis, and other fields due to their simple preparation process, large surface area, and high luminescence (Liu et al., 2022). One of the key advantages of CQDs is their low toxicity, which makes them environmentally friendly. And they exhibit stable fluorescence properties, ensuring consistent performance over time. Furthermore, the preparation of CQDs is relatively simple, making them accessible for a wide range of applications. It is used as an alternative to traditional natural enzymes in quantitative and qualitative food analysis, providing a simple, stable, sensitive, and specific method for rapid detection in the field of food analysis (Solaimuthu, Vijayan, Murali, & Korrapati, 2020; Yao et al., 2022). However, most CQDs were prepared by using chemical reagents. This not only necessitates a huge quantity of raw materials and reagents, which raises costs, but the synthesis process frequently generates wastewater, exhaust fumes, and other harmful compounds. As a result, the green preparation of CQDs has become a research focus. Among them, corn, as one of the major food crops in China, is a natural cellulose material with the advantages of being green and non-toxic, low cost, and one of the ideal materials for the preparation of biomass CQDs. The presence of polysaccharides such as cellulose in corncobs enriches the functional groups on the surface of CQDs thus enhancing the photoluminescence of CQDs (Guan, Wang, & Sun, 2022; Zhang, Wang, Liu, Ni, & Hou, 2019). The water solubility and abundance of functional groups on the surface of the prepared CQDs facilitate the bonding of CQDs with ∙OH to produce a fluorescence burst. As food waste, sunflower seed husk and grape seed are also carbon sources for the production of CQDs and perform similar functions to corncob (Lin et al., 2022; Tan, Ren, Xu, Zhu, & Li, 2023).

In this study, fluorescent CQDs with good optical properties and catalytic activity were prepared from three types of food wastes, namely corncob, sunflower seed husk and grape seed, using a green synthesis method, and a fluorescence ratio and colorimetric method for detecting DH with high sensitivity and selectivity were developed. CQDs has strong peroxidase mimetic activity, catalyzing the oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) by H2O2 and resulting in a colorimetric reaction. While DH uses its reducibility to reduce the degree of oxidation of ABTS, so that the system fades. Furthermore, CQDs can react with H2O2 to make ·OH, which bursts the fluorescence of CQDs itself, but ·OH reacts with DH to form OH−, which reduces the degree of the fluorescence burst on CQDs, restoring the fluorescence. As a result, the dual nature of catalytic fluorescence based on CQDs allows for the effective quantitative detection of DH (Scheme 1 ). In this work, CQDs was synthesized as a new fluorescent nano-mimetic enzyme that may be used instead of traditional enzymes for quantitative and qualitative examination of food. This provides some theoretical basis for improved food detection techniques and broadens the application of fluorescent nanomaterials mimicking enzymes in colorimetric and fluorescent detection methods.

DH, acetic acid, sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium acetate were purchased from Yongda Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). H2O2 (3%), ABTS, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and NaOH were purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was purchased from Yaohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Phosphate buffer was purchased from Fuzhou Feijing Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Fujian, China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and required no further purification. Sweet corn sticks, raw sunflower seeds, Kyoho grapes, pork loin, pork heart, pork liver and pork lung were purchased from Darunfa Supermarket (Harbin, China). Deionized water was used during the experiments.

CQDs was synthesized by the following instruments: YZHR-25 hydrothermal synthesis reactor and DK-98-1 electrothermal constant temperature water bath pot (Xintai Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China); Electronic balance (Nicolet Co., Ltd., Madison, Wisconsin, USA); PHS-2F pH meter, TD5A centrifuge and KQ-100VDB ultrasonic cleaner (Foheng Instrument Co., Ltd., Jieyang City, Guangdong Province, China). CQDs were characterized by Magna-IR 560 fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet Co., Ltd., Madison, Wisconsin, USA), HT-7700 Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), and ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). In addition, the fluorescence and UV detection of CQDs were performed using FP-HORIBA FluoroMax-4 fluorometer (Guojia Hengye Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) and UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Xintai Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

The corncob was cut and crushed with a grinder to pass through a 60 mesh sieve, and the sieved corncob powder was dried at 60 °C for 48 h. In a 50 mL beaker, 0.6 g of corncob powder and 30 mL of deionized water were sequentially added, mixed and allowed to marinate for 30 min before being placed in a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor (200 °C) for 6 h. To obtain the fluorescent CQDs suspension, the black-brown reaction solution was centrifuged at 2200 g for 5 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane. The suspension has been dehydrated to a powder at 60 °C and stored at 4 °C to prepare CFCD.

The grape seeds were extracted using forceps, washed with deionized water, and crushed to 60 mesh. The powder was dried at 60 °C for 48 h. In a 50 mL beaker, 1.2 g of grape seed powder and 30 mL of deionized water were added and steeped for 30 min. The following stages of preparation were identical to those for CFCD, resulting in grape seed fluorescent carbon quantum dots (GFCD).

After the sunflower seed husks were removed, they were soaked for 1.5 h in a 0.5% NaOH solution. This method was utilized to get rid of the remaining lipid components from the sunflower seed husk. After soaking, rinsing with deionized water, and crushing to 60 mesh, the remaining preparation procedure can be comparable to that of GFCD, and sunflower seed husk fluorescent carbon quantum dots (SFCD) were prepared.
The prepared fluorescent CQDs were characterized by fluorescence, UV, FT-IR, spectroscopy and XPS.

Four stability experiments on three fluorescent CQDs were performed. Three milliliters of each of the three prepared samples were scanned for fluorescence, and the characteristic peaks and their respective wavelengths were recorded. First, for acid-base stability, HCl solution (1 mol/L) and NaOH solution (1 mol/L) were used to adjust the pH of the samples to 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8 in order to ascertain the stability of the three fluorescent CQDs at varying pH. Second, to test for work stability, three fluorescent CQDs were placed in 3 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 mol/L, pH 4) and heated in a 50 °C water immersion for varying amounts of time. Thirdly, salt concentration stability was determined by adding NaCl to 3 mL of the test sample to ensure that the final NaCl concentrations in the system were 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.25 and 1.50 mol/L. After 10 min, the mixture was homogenized and observed while being monitored. Lastly, stability during storage. The three fluorescent CQDs were sealed and stored at 4 °C for 1, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 days while being routinely monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and steady-state kinetics of fluorescent CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three fluorescent CQDs were subjected to UV for substantiation of simulated enzymatic activity using the H2O2-ABTS system. The chromogenic substrate was ABTS, the oxidizing substrate was H2O2 and the three fluorescent CQDs were used as catalysts. The change in absorbance at 420 nm was utilized as an indicator for determining the viability of the peroxidase mimics.

Varying the concentration of the substrate TMB or H2O2 over a range of concentrations was used as a method to evaluate the enzyme steady-state kinetics of fluorescent CQDs. Double inverse plots were created by concentration changes and reaction kinetic parameters were calculated. To be specific, the entire reaction was carried out at 50 °C. A fluorescent CQDs suspension (150 μL) at a concentration of 2 mg/mL was added to 3 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4). For one thing, the substrate for the kinetic analysis of the enzymatic reaction of TMB was H2O2 (10 mmol/L), and the concentration of the TMB buffer was changed. Secondly, for the kinetic analysis of the enzymatic reaction of H2O2, 10 mmol/L of TMB was used as a substrate, and the concentration of the H2O2 buffer was varied. The reaction kinetic parameters were obtained by scanning the system with a UV spectrophotometer at 652 nm for the first 5 min of the reaction (Lee, 2018; Rastogi, Karunasagar, Sashidhar, & Giri, 2017).

Concentrations of 10 mmol/L of ABTS solution (200 μL), 150 μL of CQDs (2.0 mg/mL) and 500 μL of different concentrations of H2O2 (5, 10, 50, 100 and 200 mmol/L) were added sequentially to 3 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 mol/L, pH 4). The reaction systems were incubated at different temperatures (30, 40, 50, 60 and 70 °C) in water containers for 20 min before being removed and gently inverted every 1 min. The system was left for 1 min before 3 mL were aspirated for absorbance measurements at 420 nm, with distilled water serving as a blank. After adding 200 μL of DH solution (0.1 mmol/L) for varied times (10, 25, 40, 55 and 70 s), the absorbance at 420 nm was measured once more. The test was repeated three times, and the difference between the absorbance before and after the reaction, denoted by the symbol ΔA was calculated and used as an indicator, with a larger value indicating a more favorable outcome.

Different concentrations of H2O2 (5, 10, 50, 100 and 200 mmol/L) were added to 500 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 mol/L, pH 4.0), while various volumes of 2.0 mg/mL CQDs suspensions (50, 100, 150, 200 and 250 μL) were added. The reaction system was deposited in a water bath at various temperatures (30, 40, 50, 60 and 70 °C) and incubated for 20 min before being removed and gently inverted every 1 min. After incubation, 3 mL were aspirated to determine the fluorescence intensity F
0 . The fluorescence intensity F was measured 3 min after adding 150 μL of a 0.1 mmol/L DH solution. The test was repeated three times, and the absorbance ΔF difference was used as an indicator.

The optimal detection system was obtained based on optimization experiments in the UV system. 0.001–1.300 mmol/L DH working curves were plotted against ΔA, and the minimum detection limits were also obtained through the working curves. Based on the fluorescence detection system, the working curves of DH with ΔF in the range of 0.001–1.000 mmol/L were plotted and the minimum detection limits were also obtained from the working curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the dual sensing detection system
ce:section.ce:para: As a key indicator of the test system, the interference resistance of the assay was utilized. Anti-interference investigations were performed using a dual colorimetric/fluorescent sensor designed to imitate enzyme CQDs. Substances commonly found in foods such as NaCl, KCl, glycine (Gly), threonine (Thr), leucine (Leu), l-cysteine (L-Cys), sucrose (Suc) and fructose (Fru) were used as interfering substances. And the color and fluorescence intensities of the 0.10 mmol/L DH solution and interfering compounds were evaluated. Among them, the concentration of interfering substances was 100 times that of DH concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DH in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed sensor was utilized to quantify DH in animal product samples. Actual testing samples consisted of pork loin, pork heart, pork liver and pork lung procured from regular sources, and the pretreatment referred to the method of Li, Gan, Yang, Fu, and Wang (2021). The actual sample of 1.0 g was weighed and mixed in 50 mL of 0.01 mol/L phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) before being deposited in a high-speed dispersion device for 5 min of vortexing. The suspensions were then centrifuged at 4 °C for 5 min at 4500 g and the supernatant was retained. The supernatant was adjusted to a pH of 7.4 with a 2 mol/L NaOH solution, allowed to stand for 5 min, and then frozen at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

The data obtained in the experiment were processed by Origin 2018 statistical software (Origin 2018, OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, Massachusetts, USA), three replicate samples were prepared for each sample, and the calculated and measured values are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. The mean values must be analyzed using ANOVA. Statistical analysis was performed using statistical analysis software (SPSS 19.0, IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA).

In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1 a), the stretching vibrations at 1019, 1592, 2817 and 1583 cm−1 may originate from C–H, CC, C–H and CO bonds, respectively. In detail, for CFCD, the characteristic CC expansion vibration absorption peak was shown at 1592 cm−1. The characteristic absorption peaks of C–H and O–H were at 2817 and 3327 cm−1. The absorption peak of the C–H bending vibration was 1019 cm−1. For GFCD and SFCD, the FT-IR spectrum results were similar to those of CFCD. The infrared spectra of the three fluorescent CQDs showed that the three fluorescent CQDs prepared contain hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, making them all good hydrophilic and biocompatible (Al-Azzam & Alazzam, 2022).

The three produced fluorescent CQDs were scanned using a UV spectrophotometer at 275–450 nm wavelengths, respectively. Fluorescence spectroscopy was used to scan the emission spectra of three fluorescent CQDs at various excitation wavelengths. The optimal emission wavelengths for CFCD, GFCD, and SFCD were 475, 550, and 477 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of the three fluorescent carbon quantum dots were scanned at optimal emission wavelengths of 450∼600 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra as well as the fluorescence spectra of the three fluorescent CQDs were demonstrated by Fig. 1b and c. As can be seen from Fig. 1b, there is a distinct UV absorption peak at 312, 330 and 310 nm for 2 mg/mL of CFCD, 4 mg/mL of GFCD and 4 mg/mL of SFCD, respectively. The appearance of the peak was attributed to π→π* in CC and n→π* in CO. Therefore, it can be concluded that the surface states of the three fluorescent CQDs prepared absorb the energy of the excited state. From this speculation, the optical properties of the three fluorescent CQDs are primarily determined by the luminescence of the surface defect state, the size of the CQDs, the surface functional groups, and the oxygen-containing groups. As can be seen in Fig. 1c, CFCD, GFCD, and SFCD were shown to have a distinct fluorescence emission peak at 475, 550 and 477 nm, respectively. Among them, the peak fluorescence intensity of CFCD was much higher than that of GFCD and SFCD. This indicates that the molecular structure of the p-p conjugated double bond of CFCD was more when compared to the other two fluorescent CQDs. Because the amount of p-electron conjugation determines the amount of fluorescence produced, increasing the amount increases the intensity. It is possible to provide a rational explanation for the observation that the degree of p-electron conjugation of CFCD was significantly higher than that of the other two fluorescent CQDs (Wang et al., 2023). The vast majority of substances that include aromatic or heterocyclic rings have the ability to emit fluorescence. In terms of the influence that substituents have on the fluorescence that is emitted by the molecule, the degree of p-conjugation increases when a portion of the oxygen-containing functional group is substituted by an electronic group. As a result, the fluorescence intensity also increases. For example, –CH3, –OH, and –OR were in accordance with the above-mentioned pattern, and it was inferred that CFCD had a high number of oxygen-containing functional groups (Bajer & Kaczmarek, 2022).

Based on the mock validation experiments for enzyme activity, peroxidase-catalyzed H2O2 caused a color change in ABTS, which served as the premise for the validation experiments. Fig. 1d illustrates the comparison of the different compositional factors in the catalytic reaction. Three of the systems, the CFCD-H2O2 system, the CFCD-ABTS system and the H2O2-ABTS system solution system, exhibited neither obvious absorption peaks nor obvious color variations. Only in the presence of CFCD, H2O2 and ABTS does the reaction occur, and the solution of the reaction system appears green (curve d). At 420 nm, the CFCD–H2O2–ABTS system exhibited a significant UV absorption peak. GFCD and SFCD produced comparable results to CFCD (Fig. 1e and f). Thus, it was hypothesized that each of the three fluorescent CQDs possesses peroxidase activity comparable to that of horseradish peroxidase.

The XPS spectra show the elemental and chemical composition of the CQDs. Among them, Fig. 2 a shows the full XPS spectrum of CFCD. The measured spectra revealed the presence of C and O elements in CFCD. Fig. 2b shows the high resolution spectrum of C1s. There were mainly two different characteristic peaks. The peak at 284.39 eV belonged to the binding energy of the CC bond, and the peak at 285.85 eV could correspond to the C–O bond. Fig. 2c presents the high-resolution spectrum of O1s. The binding energies of 531.65 and 532.78 eV could be attributed to C–OH and C–O, respectively. Based on the above analysis, the results of XPS and FT-IR tests were consistent with each other, which once again demonstrated the successful preparation of CQDs from corncobs. Meanwhile, the XPS allometric maps of the CQDs prepared from grape seed and sunflower seed husk are shown in Fig. 2d and g, respectively. Fig. 2e shows the high-resolution spectra of C1s of CQDs prepared from grape seeds. 284.42 eV was the binding energy of the CC bond, 284.94 eV could correspond to the binding energy of the C–O, and the characteristic peak at 285.83 eV was the characteristic peak of the CO. Fig. 2f shows the high-resolution spectra of O1s of CQDs prepared from grape seeds. The binding energies of 531.95, 532.77 and 533.75 eV may be attributed to C–OH, CO, and C–O, respectively. Fig. 2h shows the high-resolution spectra of C1s of CQDs prepared from sunflower seed husks. 284.28 eV was the binding energy of the CC bond, and the characteristic peak at 285.64 eV was the characteristic peak of CO. Fig. 2i shows the high-resolution spectra of O1s of CQDs prepared from sunflower seed husks The spectrum exhibits three characteristic peaks of 532.02, 532.47 and 532.67 eV, whose binding energies may be attributed to C–OH, C–O and C–O, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that CQDs prepared from these three plant wastes have hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl groups, making them both hydrophilic and biocompatible. This was in agreement with the results of other characterization methods and also indicated the feasibility of successfully preparing fluorescent CQDs from these three plant wastes.

The stability of fluorescent nanoparticles under various physiological conditions was an important feature for their practical application. The pH, work stability, salt concentration and storage stability were selected as important indicators of the stability of fluorescent nanoparticles (Fig. 3 ). Between pH 3 and 8, GFCD exhibited the greatest degree of stability compared to CFCD and SFCD, with the latter exhibiting the least amount of stability (Fig. 3a). The experimental results of working stability show that the performance of CFCD is significantly better than that of GFCD and SFCD (Fig. 3b). The stability of the three CQDs was not significantly affected by the salt content, which was one of the four parameters that were considered (Fig. 3c). The fluorescence intensity of all three CQDs decreased with increasing storage time (Fig. 3d). However, the fluorescence intensity of CFCD decreased by less than 10 percent. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity values of GFCD and SFCD decreased significantly under identical conditions. It is possible that the fluorescence of GFCD and SFCD was more sensitive to UV light, and that the fluorescence of some fluorescent substances would diminish after prolonged exposure to UV light and air oxidation. In addition, oxygen is the most common fluorescence bursting agent. After storage for a certain period of time, the oxygen in the system and the CQDs form non-luminous ground state complexes, thus bursting the fluorescence. Among them, the fluorescence peaks of GFCD and SFCD values were lower than those of CFCD, and the effect of oxygen on them may be more significant (Abdulkhaleq et al., 2023). Therefore, it can be concluded that CFCD has better storage stability over a period of up to 50 days. All the above results indicate that CFCD prepared by one-step hydrothermal greening exhibits great stability under various conditions.

By comparing the XPS, FT-IR, UV, fluorescence and stability of the three CQDs, the overall conditions of the green-generated CFCD were superior to the other two CQDs. Therefore, CFCD was selected for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 4 a and b revealed that the fluorescent CFCD possessed prominent nanostructures. CFCD nanoparticles exhibited exceptional dispersion, lacked a discernible aggregation phenomenon, and possessed spherical particles with a particle size of approximately 4.6 ± 0.45 nm. This matches the CQDs dimensions. In addition, the steady-state kinetics of CFCD-simulated enzyme catalysis were investigated and depicted in Fig. 4c and d. Fig. 4c demonstrates that when the concentration of TMB remained constant and it was used as the substrate, the initial rate of the reaction increased with increasing H2O2 concentration before leveling off and becoming stable progressively. When the concentration of H2O2 remained constant as the substrate, the initial rate of the reaction increased with increasing TMB concentration before leveling off and becoming stable progressively (Fig. 4d). This is consistent with the Michaelis-Menten equation (Sun et al., 2022).

The kinetic parameters of CFCD and other catalysts are shown in Table 1 . In the enzyme-substrate binding capacity, the Michaelis constant (K m) was inversely proportional to the affinity capacity (Bilalis, Karagouni, & Toubanaki, 2022). As shown in Table 1, the K m values of the CFCD mimetic enzyme with H2O2 and TMB as substrates are significantly lower than those of other nanomaterials having peroxidase activity. This indicates that the synthesized CFCD catalyzes the reaction more quickly, in less time, with greater efficiency, and with greater affinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultraviolet detection DH based on CFCD mimetic enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: In the CFCD enzyme-catalyzed system, temperature, reaction time and the concentration of H2O2 were thought to be critical parameters effecting the detection of DH. As the first factor to be investigated (Fig. 5 a), temperature was chosen. The absorbance difference increased and subsequently decreased as the temperature went from 30 to 70 °C, with the maximum absorbance difference occurring at 40 °C. As a result, temperature was found to have a substantial impact on the catalytic activity of CFCD mimic enzymes. This is because the catalytic activity of the CFCD mimetic enzyme gradually increases with increasing temperature, leading to an increasing release of ·OH from the oxidative decomposition of H2O2 and a consequent increase in the absorbance difference. However, the catalytic activity and stability of the CFCD mimic enzyme would be reduced if the temperature was too high. This would result in a slower reaction rate and, thus, a smaller absorbance differential (Sun, Zhou, Ren, & Qu, 2018).

The amount of time needed to react was the second component that was evaluated (Fig. 5b). The rate of the system's reaction was rapid, and it was demonstrated that the absorbance difference would first increase and then reduce somewhere between 10 and 70 s. At the 40 s mark, the disparity in absorbance was at its greatest. Because the reaction time of the system was stretched out over a longer period of time, the quality of the reaction that took place improved, and the absorbance difference value increased. When the period was too long, the DH might have been oxidized by the oxygen in the air because of the lengthy exposure of DH in the air. As a result of this, the DH pure content was reduced, the reducing capacity decreased, and the absorbance change decreased. In other words, the stability of DH might be influenced by the UV radiation found in the natural environment, which would result in it gradually losing its capacity to deoxidize.

The concentration of H2O2 was the final component that was considered (Fig. 5c). The system displayed a somewhat increasing trend in the H2O2 concentrations ranging from 5 to 200 mmol/L, with the absorbance difference exhibiting both a sudden increase and a steady reduction. The reason for the increase in the absorbance difference was because there was a considerable increase in the number of ·OH that was formed by the oxidation of H2O2 by CFCD mimetic enzymes in the system. As a result, the number of ABTS molecules that were in the oxidized state increased, the color got darker, and the absorbance difference became more pronounced. Two different factors were responsible for the slow but steady reduction in the absorbance differential. On the one hand, the amino and phenolic hydroxyl groups in the DH structure were responsible for consuming the H2O2 that was present in the system. As a result, the degree to which ABTS was oxidized was reduced, the system's green color became less vibrant, and the absorbance difference value decreased (Zhang et al., 2017). On the other hand, the more H2O2 there was, the faster its own rate of decomposition was, and the less H2O2 there was that effectively reacted with the CFCD mimic enzyme. This was the case regardless of how high the quantity of H2O2 was.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection DH based on CFCD mimetic enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: In the fluorescence assay system based on CFCD simulated enzyme activity, H2O2 concentration, reaction temperature and CFCD incorporation were selected for system optimization. Fig. 6 a shows that the fluorescence intensity difference increases gradually for the H2O2 concentration range between 1 and 50 mmol/L. The reason was that the number of ·OH produced by the CFCD mimic enzyme in the system increased dramatically, and the fluorescence of CFCD was rapidly burst by the large amount of ·OH, resulting in a larger fluorescence intensity difference. When the H2O2 concentration was in the range of 50∼200 mmol/L, the fluorescence intensity difference showed a decreasing trend. This is because as the concentration of H2O2 in the system increases, the ·OH produced by CFCD mimetic enzyme catalysis reacts with the excess H2O2 and reduces the degree of fluorescence burst of CFCD (Ray, Biswas, Banerjee, & Biswas, 2020).

As can be observed in Fig. 6b, the temperature at which the reaction was carried out also had a significant impact on the difference in fluorescence intensity. The temperature at which the highest difference in fluorescence intensity was recorded was 40 °C, which also suggested that the catalytic activity of the CFCD mimic enzyme was at its peak at this temperature. This is because with the increasing temperature, the catalytic activity of the CFCD mimetic enzyme is increasing, the oxidative decomposition efficiency of H2O2 is accelerated, the ·OH produced is increasing, and the difference in fluorescence intensity is increasing. While too high a temperature accelerates the oxidative decomposition that occurs in H2O2 itself, leading to a decrease in the number of ·OH in the system, this results in a decreasing trend in the difference in fluorescence intensity (Liu et al., 2019). As a result, temperature can impact the catalytic activity of the CFCD mimetic enzyme and the stability of H2O2 (Zhou et al., 2022).

It can be seen from Fig. 6c that the excessive addition of CFCD is not beneficial. The difference in fluorescence intensity in the fluorescence detecting system did not always indicate a rising trend with the steady rise in the amount of CFCD supplied. The difference in fluorescence intensity reached its highest at 150 μL of CFCD addition. The reason may be that the catalytic activity of CFCD is proportional to the amount of CFCD added within a certain range. Following that, it showed a diminishing tendency as CFCD addition increased. This might be owing to the fluorescent chemicals' self-absorption and internal filtering effects when the CFCD addition exceeds a certain threshold, resulting in a drop in fluorescence intensity (Xu et al., 2020). As a result, the concentration of fluorescent chemicals used in fluorescence measurements should not be excessively high.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-read assay for DH quantitation based on CFCD mimetic enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: The linear range and limit of detection of a quantitative test for DH based on CFCD mimetic enzyme activity were investigated under optimum parameters specified at single factor. The hue of the solution progressively changed from green to pea green when viewed with the naked eye in daylight. As a result, the absorbance at 452 nm of differences in the UV–vis spectra increased (Fig. 7 a). Meanwhile, the fluorescent color shifted from blue to oxford blue in the presence of increasing DH concentrations, resulting in a progressive increase in emission strength at 475 nm (Fig. 7b).

In Fig. 7a, ΔA (ΔA = A−A 0, A 0 and A represented the absorbance of the biosensor with and without DH, respectively) against their corresponding DH concentrations had a good linear relationship across a range of 0.001–0.700 mmol/L. This resulted in a linear regression equation of y = 0.8927x+0.2184 (R 2 = 0.9952), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.93 μmol/L. Another calibration plot was produced by fluorescent absorption spectra for the sensing platform as well (Fig. 7b). The fluorescence intensity differences displayed linear correlations with the corresponding DH concentrations between 0.001 and 0.600 mmol/L, with a linear regression equation of y = 1039.2x+171.99 (R 2 = 0.9947). According to 3σ/s (s was the slope of the curve while σ was the standard deviation), the LOD was estimated to be 7.86 nmol/L.

As shown in Table 2 , the fluorometric method used in this study to detect DH had relatively low detection limits compared to previously reported assays. The UV–vis colorimetric method had lower detection limits than the majority of assays but higher detection limits than a few assays. It indicated that the colorimetric/fluorescence dual sensing system for the determination of DH based on the simulated enzyme activity of CFCD had high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CFCD based simulated enzyme activity detection system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed biosensor for DH quantification in samples, its response to common food substances was examined. Under ideal experimental conditions, each interfere was introduced separately to the system in the presence and absence of DH. where the concentration of the interfering substance was 100 times the concentration of DH. The results were then determined using F/F 0 and the absorbance at 452 nm. Fig. 8 a demonstrates that only when the DH standard solution was introduced to the reaction system did a significant color intensity response value appear, showing that only DH has the ability to suppress ABTS oxidation, effectively fading the green color. The results in Fig. 8b could likewise confirm that the fluorescence bursting caused by the CFCD mimic enzyme is likely to resurface only in the presence of DH. In conclusion, the proposed method was created to detect DH in food with a high level of selectivity and practicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of determining the utility of the biosensor, different types of pork meat samples were collected for DH analysis. Take 1 mL of the actual sample solution after pre-treatment and add an equal amount of DH standard solution (0.020, 0.100 and 0.200 mmol/L) so that the spiked concentrations of the solutions are 0.010, 0.050 and 0.100 mmol/L, respectively. Then sonicate for 15 min. The mixture (150 μL) was taken and added to the optimal detection system to determine the spiked recovery. The results of DH testing on porcine meat are presented in Table 3 . Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption methods yielded recoveries ranging from 96.94 to 105.27% and 94.99–103.24% for various swine meat samples, respectively. The RSD of all actual samples measured by both techniques was less than 3.77%, indicating that the dual-read CFCD biosensor was reliable for DH detection.

A one-step hydrothermal approach was used to successfully create three bifunctional CQDs with peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent characteristics from food waste (corncob, sunflower seed husk, and grape seed). Three CQDs characteristics are examined. Among them, fluorescent CQDs generated from corncob have shown evident benefits and established themselves as a high-quality material for green fluorescent CQDs production. The CFCD mimetic enzyme efficiently catalyzes both ABTS-H2O2 to produce a chromogenic reaction and H2O2 to produce ·OH. While DH can not only reduce the above chromogenic products but also react with the fluorescence quencher ·OH to restore the fluorescence of CFCD. As a result, a dual colorimetric/fluorescent sensor was developed using CFCD's peroxidase activity and fluorescent characteristics to detect DH in food quantitatively. The results revealed that the sensing platform has the benefits of a wide detection linear range, a low detection limit, excellent stability, and strong selectivity, and it was effectively used to detect DH in real-world samples. In summary, CFCDs prepared by green synthesis are low-cost, simple to operate and have excellent optical properties and catalytic activity, which makes them show great potential in the field of biosensing. Also, the results of this paper would provide a feasible strategy for the conversion of biomass waste, such as cellulose polysaccharides into high quality fluorescent nanomaterials.

Yanyu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Huanan Guan: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Shiqin Du: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Dandan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Qing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.
